questions.q: What is the primary purpose of access modifiers like private, protected, and public?
options: To control visibility and restrict access to class members
To improve code execution speed
To automatically document code with comments
To organize code into different files
questions.explanation: Access modifiers control which parts of a class can be accessed from outside the class, enforcing encapsulation. They have no effect on execution speed and are not related to documentation or file organization.

questions.q: In inheritance, what does a derived class inherit from its base class?
options: Only public methods and properties
Only private members
Public and protected members, but not private members
All members including private ones with full access
questions.explanation: A derived class inherits public and protected members from its base class but cannot directly access private members. Private members exist in the derived class but are only accessible through inherited public/protected methods.

questions.q: Which of the following best illustrates composition over inheritance?
options: A Car class that extends Vehicle class
A Car class that contains an Engine object rather than inheriting from Engine
A Car class with multiple constructors
A Car class that implements multiple interfaces
questions.explanation: Composition uses an object to achieve behavior flexibility, while inheritance creates a rigid hierarchy. The Car contains an Engine (composition) rather than inheriting from it, providing better flexibility and code reuse.

questions.q: What problem can deep inheritance hierarchies cause?
options: Increased memory usage proportional to depth
Fragile base class problem and tight coupling between parent and child classes
Automatic performance degradation at runtime
Compiler errors that cannot be fixed
questions.explanation: Deep hierarchies create tight coupling where changes to base classes can break derived classes unpredictably (fragile base class problem). This makes maintenance difficult, not due to memory or compilation issues.

questions.q: How does encapsulation improve code maintainability?
options: It removes the need for testing
It allows internal implementation details to change without affecting external code
It automatically prevents all bugs from occurring
It reduces the number of classes needed in an application
questions.explanation: Encapsulation hides implementation details behind a stable interface, allowing internal changes without impacting code that uses the class. This separation of concerns makes refactoring and maintenance much safer.

questions.q: When should you use composition instead of inheritance?
options: Only for mathematical operations
When objects have a 'has-a' relationship rather than an 'is-a' relationship
Whenever a class needs multiple methods
Only in languages that don't support inheritance
questions.explanation: Composition is appropriate for 'has-a' relationships (Dog has-a Tail) while inheritance suits 'is-a' relationships (Dog is-a Animal). Using composition for true 'has-a' scenarios increases flexibility and reduces coupling.

questions.q: What is a 'protected' access modifier used for?
options: To make members accessible only within the same method
To allow access only from other classes in the same package
To allow access from derived classes and the same package, but not unrelated classes
To make members completely inaccessible
questions.explanation: Protected members are accessible from derived classes and from classes in the same package, but not from unrelated classes. This enables controlled access for inheritance hierarchies while maintaining encapsulation from external code.

questions.q: Consider a Banking system where you have Account, SavingsAccount, and CheckingAccount classes. If SavingsAccount and CheckingAccount both extend Account, but they behave differently in critical ways (e.g., overdraft handling), what design risk exists?
options: The code will not compile
Both derived classes are forced to override every method from the base class
Code expecting an Account reference may assume behavior that doesn't hold for all subclasses, violating the Liskov Substitution Principle
The derived classes will automatically merge into a single class
questions.explanation: When subclasses violate LSP by behaving differently in ways callers don't expect, code that treats them polymorphically may fail. For example, code assuming all Accounts allow overdrafts would break with CheckingAccount.

questions.q: Why might a class prefer using getter and setter methods instead of public fields?
options: Getters and setters are faster than direct field access
Getters and setters allow validation, side effects, and implementation changes without affecting callers
Public fields automatically provide thread safety
It reduces the total lines of code in a program
questions.explanation: Encapsulation through getters and setters allows the class to add validation, trigger side effects, or change internal representation later without breaking external code that depends on the interface.

questions.q: In a scenario where you need shared behavior across multiple unrelated classes, why is composition with an interface often better than multiple inheritance?
options: Multiple inheritance is always illegal in programming languages
Composition is less flexible than multiple inheritance
Composition avoids the complexity and conflicts of multiple inheritance while still achieving code reuse through delegation
Interfaces cannot define any behavior at all
questions.explanation: Composition with interfaces avoids problems like the diamond problem and ambiguous method resolution that occur with multiple inheritance, while still enabling shared behavior through delegation and implementation of common interfaces.
